baseStepTitles: Build the back as a flat panel
Drop in the back, cleats, slats
flags: CONTRADICTION: the two 2x2 seat cleats are 54-1/2" but the step says to fit them 'inside the frame', whose interior is 50-1/2" (bases, back rail and back rest top are all 50-1/2"). 54-1/2" is the OUTSIDE figure — it matches the back base, which overhangs 2" at each end (54-1/2 − 4 = 50-1/2). The cleats are either not an interior part or the length is wrong; the prose says so and does not pick a side.

The eight seat slats do not deck the seat in either direction: eight 3-1/2" slats total 28" against a 50-1/2" interior width, and at 23-1/2" long they are too short to span that width the other way. Slat direction and gap are not determined by the plan's data.
Back-slat spacing (about 5-7/8" between the six 3-1/2" slats on the 50-1/2" rail) is DERIVED arithmetic, not an authored value.
Lumber verified sufficient with the packing script: five 8 ft 2x4, three 8 ft 2x6, three 5 ft 2x2 and two 8 ft 1x4 — exactly the declared quantities. No shortfall.

ops.body: Still working upside down on the flat armrest, set the 31" 2x4 side support between the two legs of each arm and pocket-screw it at both ends. That 31" length is what sets the arm's depth: 31" of support plus a 1-1/2" leg at each end comes to 34", which is exactly the armrest's length, so the legs finish flush with both ends of the armrest. Add the 31" 2x6 end apron to each arm, flush to the outside face, with its pocket holes on the inside and a second row of holes drilled along one edge for attaching the seat structure in the next step. Keep every pocket hole either on an inside face or pointing down — that is the collection's finish standard and there is no hiding one later.

Stand the two arm assemblies upright and parallel and connect them with the two 50-1/2" 2x6 bases and the 50-1/2" 2x4 back rail, pocket holes at every end, screwed with the 2-1/2" exterior pocket screws. Those three parts are all the same length on purpose: they fix the seat interior at 50-1/2", which puts the finished loveseat at about 53-1/2" across the outside faces of the arms. Measure both diagonals across the seat box before the last screws — a box that is out of square here will not accept the flat back panel you build next. Check your cushions against that 50-1/2" interior now, while changing the base and rail lengths is still one saw cut rather than a rebuild.

On sawhorses, lay the six 23-3/4" 2x4 back slats out under the 50-1/2" 2x2 top rail and set the two outer slats flush with the rail's ends first, then space the middle four between them. Six slats at 3-1/2" wide use 21" of the 50-1/2" rail, so the five gaps between them come to 29-1/2" total — about 5-7/8" each — and marking all five before you drive anything is what makes the spacing read as machine-even. Fasten each slat to the top rail with 2-1/2" screws, then screw the other end of every slat to the 54-1/2" 2x6 back base, positioned so the base overhangs 2" past the outermost slat at each end (54-1/2 less two 2" overhangs leaves exactly the 50-1/2" the slats occupy). Square the panel with a diagonal check before you carry it to the frame; a racked back panel will fight the seat box in the next step.

Set the finished back panel into the seat frame at the recline angle you want, rest it on the back 2x6 base, and screw through the panel's back base into the arm assemblies and through into the 2x4 back rail with 2-1/2" screws. Now the cleats: the cut list calls for two 2x2 seat cleats at 54-1/2", but the seat frame's interior is only 50-1/2", so a 54-1/2" cleat will not fit inside it — 54-1/2" is the same outside figure as the back base, so either these are not an interior part or the length is a mistake. Decide before cutting: if the cleats run along the inside faces of the two 50-1/2" bases to carry the seat slats, they need to be cut to the 50-1/2" interior. Lay the eight 1x4 seat slats on the cleats with a gap between each for drainage and one 1-1/2" screw per joint, then sand everything, stain all faces including the undersides, and set the cushions in.

flags: 2x2 SHORTFALL (marginal but real): the two 25" end trim pieces need 50" out of the single declared 50" stick, leaving nothing for the saw kerf. Buy a slightly longer 2x2 or accept one trim piece about 1/8" short.

The plan does not state which pair of frame members runs full length, and the box-geometry solver returns no determination. The 25" end trim equals 22" plus two 1-1/2" members, which POINTS to the 22" long members sitting between the 19" short ones (a 25" x 19" footprint) — stated in the prose as a derivation, not as an authored fact.

Step 5's trim cannot do both jobs as written. With the legs flush to the frame top and the 1-1/2"-thick tabletop boards laid flat on the frame, the top surface is 1-1/2" above the leg tops, so one 1-1/2" trim piece cannot cover the leg tops and also finish flush with the tabletop. The board ends also face sideways, so screws cannot be driven 'down through the trim into the board ends'.
Tabletop gap (about 3/4" over five gaps) is DERIVED from six 3-1/2" boards inside the 25" trim length, not an authored value.
2x4 verified sufficient: three 8 ft boards, exactly as declared.

ops.body: From the 2x4, cut two long frame members at 22", two short frame members at 19", four legs at 13-1/2" and six tabletop boards at 22". Allowing about 1/8" per saw cut, all fourteen 2x4 parts pack into the three declared 8 ft boards with a little over 3" to spare, so cut the long pieces first and take the legs out of the offcuts. From the 2x2, cut the two 25" end trim pieces — note that two 25" pieces use the whole 50" stick with no allowance for the kerf, so either buy a slightly longer 2x2 or accept one piece coming up a hair short. Square every cut and keep the six 22" tabletop boards separate from the two 22" frame members; they are the same length and easy to mix up.

Lay the four frame members flat and screw them into a rectangle with two 2-1/2" exterior screws per corner, driven through the face of one member into the end grain of the other. The plan does not say which pair runs the full length, but the 25" end trim equals 22" plus a 1-1/2" member at each end, which points to the 22" pieces sitting between the 19" pieces — that gives a 25" x 19" frame. Pick one reading and use it for all four corners, then measure both diagonals; if they match, the frame is square and every later part will land where you expect it.

Stand a 13-1/2" leg in each inside corner of the frame with its top flush to the top edge of the frame, and drive two 2-1/2" screws from inside the frame into each face of the leg. Tip the bottom of each leg very slightly outward before driving the second screw — that small splay is the one real technique in this build and it does more to kill wobble than any bracket would. With the legs flush to the frame top and the 1-1/2"-thick tabletop laid on afterwards, the finished table stands about 15" tall. Stand it up on a flat floor and check it does not rock before you go any further.

Lay the six 22" tabletop boards flat across the frame, running them the short way so each board's ends land on the two long frame rails. Six boards at 3-1/2" wide cover 21", so inside the 25" length set by the trim there is about 4" of gap to share out — roughly 3/4" between each pair of boards, which drains rain and keeps the spacing looking deliberate. Dry-lay all six and adjust the gaps before any screw goes in, mark the screw positions in two straight lines so the fasteners read as a pattern, then drive two 2-1/2" screws through each board end into the rail below.

Run a 25" 2x2 along each end of the tabletop, covering the cut ends of the six boards. Be aware of what this trim can and cannot do: because the legs finish flush with the top of the frame and the tabletop boards then sit 1-1/2" on top of that, the leg tops are already buried under the tabletop, so a single 1-1/2" trim piece cannot both cap the leg tops and finish flush with the tabletop surface. The board ends face sideways, so drive the screws horizontally through the trim into the end grain of each board, or down through the trim into the frame rail beneath it — not down into the board ends, which are not under the trim. Pre-drill the 2x2 near its ends so it does not split.

Ease every edge and corner with sandpaper, especially the top, since bare arms and legs will rest on it. Apply the exterior penetrating stain or paint to all faces including the undersides, the leg bottoms and the end grain — end grain drinks water first and is where outdoor furniture starts to fail. Let it cure fully before setting a cushion on top to use it as an ottoman, or leave it bare as an accent table.
materials: Exterior penetrating wood stain or paint

baseStepTitles: Join the case top, bottom, and sides
Build the drawer boxes and install slides
Attach the drawer faces and door
flags: HEADLINE DEFECT — the cut list gives the carcass two incompatible widths and does not close. The box-geometry solver proves the 48" sides run full and the 24-3/4" case partitions sit between them, because the cabinet back is 48" x 26-1/4" (24-3/4 + two 3/4" sides) — a 26-1/4" outside, 24-3/4" clear case. But the case top is 29-3/4", the long trim boards are 29-3/4", the wide drawer boxes compute to 28-3/4" outside (confirmed by the 14" x 28-3/4" wide drawer bottoms) and the wide drawer faces are 29-1/2". Those parts describe a carcass about 3-1/2" wider than the one the back and partitions describe. The prose states the contradiction and does NOT invent a joint to absorb it.

Because of the above, the existing instructions to build boxes 'sized to their actual openings' and to hang faces with 'an even 1/8" gap' cannot both be met from the given parts — the wide faces are wider than the whole carcass in the back-panel reading.

1x3 SHORTFALL (real): the four trim boards are 1-1/2" wide and total 102-1/2" of length. A 2-1/2"-wide 1x3 rips to only ONE usable 1-1/2" strip, so one 8 ft board yields 96" of trim against 102-1/2" needed — before the four narrow drawer box fronts/backs (66" at full 2-1/2" width) come off the same board.
Step 2 refers to 'the shelf' and step 3 to 'the remaining drawer slide cabinet member'; neither part exists in the cut list. The prose uses the parts that do exist and names the gap.

Verified NOT a shortfall: the 1x6 parts pack into the declared 8 ft plus 10 ft boards (the four 27-1/4" fronts/backs on the 10-footer, the six 14" sides plus two 5" sides on the 8-footer, 95-3/4" of 96"). The 1x4 (42-1/2" of 48") and 1x8 (67" of 72") are both sufficient.

ops.body: From the 3/4" plywood sheet, rip 15-3/4"-wide strips and crosscut three case partitions at 24-3/4", one case top at 29-3/4", one small divider at 8-1/4", two sides at 48" and one cabinet door at 24-1/2". From the 1/4" plywood, cut the cabinet back at 48" x 26-1/4", two small drawer bottoms at 14" x 20-1/2", two wide drawer bottoms at 14" x 28-3/4" and one top drawer bottom at 14" x 6-1/2". Take the four wide drawer box fronts/backs at 27-1/4" from the 10 ft 1x6 and the six 14" drawer box sides plus the two 5" small sides from the 8 ft 1x6 — that second board is down to its last quarter inch, so cut it carefully and in that order. Cut the two narrow drawer faces at 21-1/4" from the 1x4, and the top face at 8" plus the two wide faces at 29-1/2" from the 1x8. The 1x3 will not cover everything asked of it: the four trim boards are 1-1/2" wide and total 102-1/2", but a 2-1/2"-wide 1x3 rips to only one usable 1-1/2" strip, so a single 8 ft board yields 96" — buy a second 1x3 before you also cut the narrow drawer fronts/backs at 14" and 19" from it.

ops.title: Read the case widths before you assemble anything
ops.body: Stop and reconcile the cut list before a single screw goes in, because it gives this carcass two different widths. The 1/4" cabinet back is 48" x 26-1/4", which only closes if the two 48" sides run the full height and the 24-3/4" case partitions sit between them — a case 26-1/4" wide outside with a 24-3/4" clear opening. But the case top and the long trim boards are 29-3/4", the wide drawer boxes come out 28-3/4" wide (their own 14" x 28-3/4" bottoms prove it) and the wide drawer faces are 29-1/2", all of which describe a case roughly three and a half inches wider. Nothing in the plan says which width is right, so decide it yourself before cutting anything else: measure the openings you actually build and size the drawer boxes and faces from those measurements, not from the cut list's wide-drawer numbers.

ops.title: Build the partition assembly
ops.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes in both ends of each 24-3/4" case partition and in the top and bottom edges of the 8-1/4" small divider, then join the partitions to each other in the stack order your drawer layout calls for using 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue. Fit the small divider between two of the partitions to create the odd top-corner opening the inset cabinet door will close. Every joint gets a square check as it is driven — Euro slides magnify an out-of-square partition into a drawer that rubs on one side and gaps on the other, and there is no fixing it after the sides go on.

Stand the partition assembly between the two 48" side panels and pull it together with 3/4" pocket holes, 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, then add the case top across the top. Keep the front edges of every partition dead flush with the front edges of the sides — the whole drawer-face plane is referenced off that front surface. Measure both diagonals across the front of the case and adjust until they match before the glue grabs; this is the square check the rest of the build depends on. Note that the case top is 29-3/4" long while the partitions are 24-3/4", so it will not sit flush between 3/4" sides — see the width contradiction flagged earlier and trim it to the case you actually built.

Face the raw plywood edges with the four 1-1/2"-wide pine trim boards — the two 21-1/2" pieces and the two 29-3/4" pieces — attached with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws from behind so no fastener shows on the front. Cut them to the case you actually built rather than to the list, since the 29-3/4" figure belongs to the wider of the plan's two conflicting carcass widths. Hold each piece flush with the case front and clamp it before driving, so it cannot creep proud and stand in the way of the drawer faces later.

Measure each of the five openings you have actually built — height and width, front and back — and size each drawer box from those numbers and from the clearance your particular Euro slides specify, because the plan's own drawer parts do not agree with its carcass width. The parts as cut give a wide box 28-3/4" across (27-1/4" fronts and backs captured between 14" sides, confirmed by the 14" x 28-3/4" bottoms), a narrow-tall box 20-1/2" across, and a small top box 6-1/2" across. Build each box square with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws, glue the 1/4" plywood bottom on, and check the diagonals of every box before the glue sets — a Euro slide will not forgive a box that is out of square. Mount each slide pair so the box front finishes 3/4" back from the case front; that recess is the whole reason the applied faces can end up in one flush plane.

With the boxes running, hold each face against its box and shim it to an even reveal on all four sides before fixing it — two strips of scrap of the same thickness under and beside the face are the easiest spacers. Fasten from inside the drawer box, through the box front into the back of the face, so nothing shows from outside. Check the wide faces against your case first: at 29-1/2" they are wider than the 26-1/4" carcass the cabinet back describes, so on that reading they cannot be hung at all and must be cut to the openings you built. Hang the 24-1/2" plywood door on the full-inset concealed hinges last, and adjust the hinge cams until its gaps match the drawer reveals.

Pull the drawers back out, set the 1/4" plywood back onto the case, and check the case's diagonals one last time — the back is what locks the carcass square, so hold it to a square case rather than nailing it to a racked one. Glue the edges and every partition it crosses, then brad-nail it off with 1-1/4" brads, working from one corner outward so the panel cannot shift. Doing this after the drawers and door are fitted is deliberate: until the back is on you can reach inside to adjust a slide or a hinge.

Turn the case on its back and fasten a caster at each bottom corner, set in far enough from the edges that its mounting plate lands on solid plywood rather than on an edge. Stand it up and roll it on a flat floor before finishing — a dresser this tall on wheels wants all four casters bearing, and shimming one is far easier now than after the finish is on. Sand every surface through the grits, iron on edge banding wherever a plywood edge stays visible, and finish.

baseStepTitles: Hang the doors and add hardware
flags: MATERIAL LINE IS WRONG: the back is declared as 1/4" plywood 12" x 36", but the cut list's Back row is 13" x 36" and the geometry requires 13" (11-1/2" horizontal members between two 3/4" sides). The box-geometry solver confirms the 36" sides run full and the 11-1/2" tops/bottoms sit between them, giving a 13" outside width. Buy the back at 13" wide.

The fridge shelf is a 1x10 (9-1/4" actual) inside an 11-1/4"-deep 1x12 case, so its back edge sits 2" forward of the back panel. The existing step's instruction to nail the back into the interior shelves cannot reach it — the prose corrects this to the two tops/bottoms and the divider.

DERIVED, not authored: the three 3/4" horizontal members leave 33-3/4" of clear front opening in the 36" sides, while the two doors total 32-1/4" (12" + 20-1/4"). The remaining 1-1/2" is the width of the bottom trim; where exactly the trim lands is not stated.
Lumber verified: the 1x12 parts total 138-3/4" plus kerfs and fit the single declared 12 ft board; the 24" of 1x2 covers both 11-1/2" trim pieces.

ops.body: From the 12 ft 1x12, cut two sides at 36", three tops/bottoms at 11-1/2", the freezer door at 12" and the fridge door at 20-1/4"; that is 138-3/4" of parts plus kerfs, so the single declared board covers it with a few inches spare. Cut the fridge shelf at 11-1/2" from the 1x10 and the two bottom trim pieces at 11-1/2" from the 1x2. The back is the one part to check before you buy: the cut list calls for 13" x 36", and 13" is the number the geometry requires (11-1/2" between two 3/4" sides), so the materials line's 12" x 36" panel is an inch too narrow. Square every cut, and label the three 11-1/2" tops/bottoms and the 11-1/2" shelf as you go — they are the same length but the shelf is 2" shallower.

The two 36" sides run the full height of the box and every horizontal member fits between them, which is why the tops/bottoms are 11-1/2" and the finished case is 13" wide, 36" tall and 11-1/4" deep. Stand the sides on edge and glue and pin the bottom, the middle divider that separates freezer from fridge, and the top between them, keeping all front edges flush; the three 3/4" members leave 33-3/4" of clear opening, and the doors at 12" and 20-1/4" account for 32-1/4" of it, so set the divider to give a 12" freezer opening above a 20-1/4" fridge opening and let the bottom trim close the last 1-1/2". Add the 9-1/4"-deep fridge shelf inside the lower compartment, glued and pinned through the sides, remembering it stops 2" short of the back of the case. Nail the two 11-1/2" bottom trim pieces across the base, glue every joint, and check the case for square by measuring the diagonals before the glue sets.

Lay the case face down, check the diagonals one more time, and set the 13" x 36" plywood back onto it — the back is what holds the case square, so square it first and nail second. Glue the edges and run 1-1/4" brads into both 36" sides, the top, the bottom and the middle divider, roughly every 4" to 6". Do not aim for the fridge shelf: it is a 9-1/4"-deep 1x10 in an 11-1/4"-deep case, so its back edge is 2" in front of the panel and any nail driven at it comes out into open air.

Measure your actual openings and trim the 12" freezer door and the 20-1/4" fridge door to leave a small even gap around each before you drill for hinges — the doors are single 11-1/4"-wide boards in a 13" case, so a little trimming is expected rather than a sign something went wrong. Bore the hinge cups and mount the euro-style inset concealed hinges, then adjust the cams until both doors sit flush and their gaps match. Add a magnetic clasp at the free edge of each door and a handle on each, positioning the freezer handle low and the fridge handle high so a small hand reaches both. Round over every edge and corner with the sander — this is a toy that will be climbed on as well as played with — then apply the child-safe finish and let it cure fully before it goes in the playroom.

flags: 2x4 SHORTFALL (verified with the packing script): the 2x4 parts are two 80" caps, one 80" center support, one 67" footboard cap and two 20" braces. Three 80" pieces each need their own 96" board and the 67" plus one 20" fills the fourth, so five boards are needed against the four declared. The existing cut step already says five; the materials list says four.

1x4 SLAT SHORTFALL (verified): eleven 60" slats out of 120" stock is one slat per board, because 60 + kerf + 60 exceeds 120". Eleven boards are needed against the five declared, and the materials note's 'two queen slats per board' is not achievable at that stock length.
Verified sufficient, no shortfall: 1x6 (seven boards), 2x2 (five boards) and 2x6 (three boards) all match their declared quantities exactly.

DERIVED arithmetic used in the prose: slat gap of about 4-1/8" (80" less eleven 3-1/2" slats leaves 41-1/2" over ten gaps); headboard vertical spacing of about 17-7/8" (79" less five 1-1/2" verticals over four gaps); the 63" footboard beam between the 80" rails gives a 63" clear width, and the 1-1/2"-wide 2x2 cleats on each side reduce that to exactly the 60" slat length.

ops.body: Cut seven headboard planks at 79" from the 1x6. From the 2x2 cut one headboard top rail at 79", five headboard verticals at 31-1/2" and two slat cleats at 80". From the 2x6 cut two side rail beams at 80" and the footboard beam at 63". From the 2x4 cut two side rail caps at 80", the footboard cap at 67", the center support at 80" and two cross braces at 20" with both ends mitered 45°, long point to long point. Cut eleven slats at 60" from the 1x4. Two quantities in the materials list come up short and are worth fixing before you start: allowing 1/8" per cut you need FIVE 8 ft 2x4 rather than four, because each of the three 80" pieces takes its own board, and you need ELEVEN 10 ft 1x4 rather than five, because two 60" slats plus a kerf will not come off a 120" board. The 1x6, 2x2 and 2x6 quantities are correct as declared at seven, five and three.

Lay the seven 79" 1x6 planks face down on a flat surface with their edges snug and their ends aligned; seven 5-1/2" planks make a panel 38-1/2" tall and 79" wide. Glue and brad the 79" 2x2 top rail across the back at the top edge, then add the five 31-1/2" verticals: one flush at each end first, then the middle three spaced between them — five 1-1/2" verticals on a 79" panel leave 71-1/2" of gap, so they land roughly every 17-7/8". Nail from the back only, so the plank face stays clean, and glue every contact so the panel behaves as one piece. Sand and apply the stain or paint now while it is flat on the bench, including the plank edges where they meet, because getting finish into those seams after the bed is standing is unpleasant work.

Stand each 80" 2x6 beam on edge and lay its 80" 2x4 cap flat on top, pushed flush to what will be the INSIDE face of the beam — that offset is the L: the 2x6 on edge is the beam that carries the load, and the 2x4 lying flat gives you a wide clean top edge and something for the mattress area to register against. Glue the joint and drive 2-1/2" screws down through the cap into the beam every 10" or so. Build the footboard the same way, except that its 67" cap is 4" longer than the 63" beam: center it so exactly 2" of cap projects past each end of the beam, and check both overhangs with a tape before the screws go in, because that reveal is a deliberate design detail and an off-center cap reads as a mistake. Prefinish all three assemblies flat, edges and undersides included.

Carry the finished sections into the bedroom and stand the two 80" side rails on edge, then set the 63" footboard beam BETWEEN them at one end and screw through the rails into its ends with 2-1/2" screws, three per joint. That gives a frame 63" clear between the rails and 80" long, which is what the 60" slats and a queen mattress want. Fasten the headboard to the other ends of the rails at the height you want the panel to sit, then measure both diagonals across the frame and shift it until they match before the last screws go home. A frame that is racked now stays racked, and the cross braces in the next step will lock the error in rather than correct it.

Set a 20" cross brace into each bottom corner of the frame with its 45° cuts bedded flat against the rail and the footboard, and drive 2-1/2" screws through the brace into both members; a square corner plus a diagonal is a triangle, and a triangle is why this bed never develops the dorm-bed wiggle. Run a 2x2 slat cleat along the inside face of each 80" rail, top edges level with each other, and screw it home every foot or so — the two 1-1/2"-wide cleats reduce the 63" clear width to exactly 60", which is why the slats are 60" long and land with full bearing on both cleats. Full size and up, add the 80" 2x4 center support down the middle with its top edge flush with the cleat tops, with optional legs beneath it. Lay the eleven slats across the cleats and centre support and screw each one down: eleven 3-1/2" slats on an 80" rail leave 41-1/2" of gap over ten spaces, about 4-1/8" each — check that against your mattress maker's slat spec first, because foam mattresses usually want tighter spacing and you may want more slats than the eleven cut here.

baseStepTitles: Attach the legs and top trim
flags: CONTRADICTION FIXED IN PROSE: the cut list and steps call for a 24" 'Top back (fixed)' in 1x2, but there is NO 1x2 in the materials list. The 1x3 has about 41-5/8" of length left over after its own parts, so the prose routes the 24" back strip to a 1-1/2" rip off the 1x3 rather than restating a material that was never bought.

2x2 SHORTFALL (marginal but real, confirmed by the packing script): four 13" legs and two 10" top trim pieces total exactly 72" out of the single declared 72" stick, leaving nothing for six saw kerfs.

DIMENSIONAL MISMATCH: the tray bottom is 11-1/4" wide (a 1x12's actual width) but the tray sides are 11-1/2" long — a 1/4" difference. The 21" front/back pieces do close correctly (19-1/2" bottom plus two 3/4" sides), so the tray reads as 21" long by about 13" deep outside.
Verified sufficient: 1x12 (19-1/2" + 24" of a 48" board), 1x4 (65-1/4" of 72") and 1x3 (54-3/8" of 96", before the added back strip).

ops.body: From the 4 ft 1x12 cut the 19-1/2" storage tray bottom and the 24" hinged top. From the 1x4 cut two tray sides at 11-1/2" and two tray front/back pieces at 21". From the 2x2 cut four legs at 13" and two top trim pieces at 10" — note that those six pieces total exactly 72" out of a 72" stick, so a second short length of 2x2 or a longer stick is needed to cover the saw kerfs. From the 1x3 cut two side cubby bottoms at 10", four side cubby sides at 4", the 11-1/2" long divider and the 6" short divider, then rip a 1-1/2"-wide strip 24" long from what is left for the fixed top-back piece: the cut list calls that part a 1x2, but no 1x2 appears in the materials, and the 1x3 has roughly 41" of length to spare. Cut four side cubby fronts/backs at 4-3/4" x 10" from the 1/4" plywood scrap. Square everything and label the two 24" parts, since the lid and the back strip are the same length but different widths.

Stand the two 11-1/2" tray sides on the ends of the 19-1/2" tray bottom, glue and pin them, then wrap the two 21" front and back pieces around the outside of the sides — 19-1/2" of bottom plus a 3/4" side at each end comes to exactly the 21" those pieces are cut to, which is the check that tells you the box is going together right. Expect a 1/4" discrepancy in the other direction: the 1x12 bottom is 11-1/4" wide against 11-1/2" sides, so pull the bottom flush to the front edge and let the difference fall at the back where the lid's hinge strip will cover it. Glue every joint before nailing — on parts this thin the glue does the holding and the brads only clamp it while it cures. Measure the diagonals across the open top and adjust before the glue sets.

Turn the tray box upside down and screw a 13" 2x2 leg into each corner with 2" screws driven down through the tray bottom into the end grain of the leg, adding glue at each. These legs are what lift the tray and open up the two cubbies underneath at the ends, so keep all four flush with the outside faces of the box or the cubby parts will not sit square. Then fit the two 10" 2x2 top trim pieces across the top of the tray and drive 2" screws through the legs into the ends of the trim, which ties the legs and trim into one rigid frame rather than four separate posts. Stand it up on a flat surface and check it does not rock.

Each end cubby is a small open box: a 10" 1x3 bottom with two 4" 1x3 sides standing on it, closed front and back by a 4-3/4" x 10" panel of 1/4" plywood. Assemble both cubbies in the space between the legs at each end of the desk, gluing and pinning the plywood to the back first so you can pull the assembly square against it, then adding the front panel. Pin the plywood every couple of inches with 1-1/4" brads and keep the nails away from the panel edges, since 1/4" ply splits easily. Beyond giving the desk two open compartments, these closed-in ends triangulate the leg frame and take most of the wobble out of the whole assembly.

The 11-1/2" long divider runs the full inside depth of the tray and the 6" short divider crosses part-way into one of the two compartments it makes. Dry-fit them first and slide them around until the compartments suit what you actually keep on a lap desk — a long channel for pens along the front and a wider bay for a phone and a notepad behind it is a good starting layout, but the plan deliberately leaves the positions to you. Once you have settled it, mark the positions on the outside of the tray sides and front, glue the divider edges, and pin through from outside with 1-1/4" brads. Dividers are what stop everything sliding into one corner every time the desk tips.

Glue and pin the 24" fixed back strip across the back of the desk with its outside edge flush, running the full 24" width so the lid has a solid fixed edge to hinge from. Lay the 24" x 11-1/4" 1x12 lid in place, mark the hinge positions on the back strip a few inches in from each end, and mount the pair of standard hinges so the lid pivots up and toward you while the tray and its contents stay put. Open and close it a few times before finishing and check that the lid clears the top trim and lands flat when shut. Sand the lid to a fine grit — it is the writing surface and the one part of this project people will judge — ease every edge, and finish.

baseStepTitles: Build the two sides — flat, in mirror
Roof panel on the ground, then up
flags: The existing cut step's board tally was wrong: it called for five 12 ft 2x4 and dropped the declared 8 ft 2x4 entirely. Verified with the packing script, the 12 ft 2x4 parts (two 72" legs, three 65-3/8" legs, two 73-1/2" and one 70-1/2" roof rails) pack into FOUR 12-footers, and the two 40" roof sides come off the 8 ft board — exactly the declared four plus one.

Verified sound: the 2x6 parts (two 28" rails, three 70-1/2" floor boards) pack into the two declared 12-footers. Picket count also closes at 22 as declared — each of 14 pickets yields one 28" side picket plus a 43" roof picket from the offcut (28 + 1/8 kerf + 43 = 71-1/8" of 72"), plus seven 71-1/2" back pickets and one 72" bottom board on their own pickets.
The steps themselves are already well specified; only the cut step was rewritten.

ops.body: From the 12 ft 2x4 cut two front legs at 72", three back legs at 65-3/8" (the third is the center back leg), two long roof rails at 73-1/2" and one center roof rail at 70-1/2"; both leg types get their top end cut at 15° off square, measured to the long point, and the 15° is what makes the roof drain to the back. Allowing 1/8" per cut those eight pieces fill four 12 ft 2x4, and the two 40" roof side pieces come off the separate 8 ft 2x4 — so it is four 12-footers plus the 8-footer, not five 12-footers. From the two 12 ft 2x6 cut two side base rails at 28" and three floor boards at 70-1/2". The pickets are the clever part of the bill: cut a 28" side picket off each of fourteen 72" pickets and the 43"-plus offcut becomes that picket's roof board, which is why fourteen pickets yield all fourteen side pickets and all fourteen roof pickets; the remaining eight pickets give seven back pickets at 71-1/2" and one bottom back board at 72". Mark the 15° cuts with the speed square and label every piece as it leaves the saw.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared

flags: The plan gives only two cross-piece lengths (two at 9-1/2" and two at 5-1/2") for three trays that are 9", 11" and 13-1/2" deep, and never states at what heights the trays land on the rails or which cross piece goes where. The prose describes the side frame from the parts that exist and stops short of asserting a tray height — that number is not in the data.
Materials are declared in board feet (1x4, 1x6 and 1x2), so no board-count check is possible and none is claimed.

Box geometry confirms the tray reading used in the prose: the 9"/11"/13-1/2" end pieces run full and the 22-1/2" sides sit between them, giving a 24" overall tray length that matches the 24" plywood bottoms exactly.

ops.body: From the 1x4 cut four tray sides at 22-1/2", two small tray ends at 9" and two medium tray ends at 11". From the 1x6 cut two large-tray sides at 22-1/2" and two large-tray ends at 13-1/2". From the 1x2 cut two straight ladder rails at 30", two angled ladder rails at 30-1/2" with BOTH ends cut at 10° off square and parallel to each other (long point to short point measurement), two small cross pieces at 9-1/2" and two large cross pieces at 5-1/2", each cross piece with one end cut at 10° measured to its longest point so it seats flush against an angled rail. Cut the three plywood bottoms 24" long and 9", 11" and 13-1/2" wide to match their trays. Set the miter saw once for the 10° and cut all six angled pieces at that setting rather than resetting between them — every one of those angles has to agree or the ladder will not lean flat against a wall.

Each tray is a shallow open box built the same way: the two end pieces run the full depth and the two 22-1/2" sides fit between them, which puts the finished tray at exactly 24" long — the length of the plywood bottoms. Drill 3/4" pocket holes in both ends of each side piece, on the face that will end up underneath, and pull the joints together with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue. Build the small tray with the two 9" ends, the medium with the 11" ends and the deep bottom tray with the 13-1/2" 1x6 ends and the 1x6 sides. Check each tray's diagonals while the glue is wet, then glue a plywood bottom onto the underside of each and fix it with 3/4" brads or staples — the bottom is what locks the tray square for good.

ops.title: Build the two ladder sides in mirror
ops.body: Each side of the ladder is a flat frame: one 30-1/2" rail with 10° cuts at both ends, which is the leaning member that meets the wall and the floor, one straight 30" rail, and the 9-1/2" and 5-1/2" cross pieces joining them, each cross piece presenting its 10° end to the angled rail so the two seat flush. Lay the first side out on the bench, mark where each cross piece lands, drill 3/4" pocket holes in the cross pieces and pull it together with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue. Then build the second side as a MIRROR of the first — lay it out facing the first rather than copying it, and drill its pocket holes on the opposite face, so that once the two frames face each other every hole ends up on the inside and none shows from outside. The plan gives only two cross pieces per side for three trays and does not say what height each tray sits at, so set the cross pieces where the tray depths suit you and record the positions before building the second side.

step.title: Hang the trays and finish
step.body: Stand the two ladder sides on edge facing each other and use a square to mark matching tray heights on the inside face of each, deepest tray at the bottom and shallowest at the top so the whole thing tapers with the lean. Because the trays are all 24" long, they set the width of the shelf themselves: hold each tray between the sides and drive 1-1/4" screws through the rails into the tray ends, two per joint, starting with the bottom tray and working up so the frame stays parallel. Check that all three trays read level to each other while the ladder is leaned at its working angle, not while it is lying flat — a leaning frame that was squared flat can still sit crooked against the wall. Sand every face and edge, ease the corners, and finish; then lean it against a wall so the angled rails bear flat and load the bottom tray first.

baseStepTitles: Build the headboard and footboard panels
Install drawer slides before boxing in the dividers
Assemble the storage-base frames and dividers
Hang the drawers and attach faces
flags: The existing cut step told the builder to buy three 8 ft Pine 1x2 and to cut the mouldings from it. The 1x2's only parts are the two 36" panel bottom trims, which is one board — and the optional mouldings have their own materials line (two 8 ft decorative moulding). Corrected in the prose.

NOT a shortfall, contrary to a mechanical read of the packer: the four 30" and four 16-3/4" mouldings hand-pack as 30 + 30 + 16-3/4 + 16-3/4 = 93-1/2" plus three kerfs on each of TWO 96" sticks. The two declared are sufficient.

The 14 drawer support blocks (7" of 1x3) are cut but never placed by any step, and the final step's mattress slats have no cut-list row at all — no slat length, count or spacing is given. Both named in the prose as builder's-choice gaps rather than invented.

GEOMETRIC GAP: the headboard/footboard panels are 36" wide and the 2x3 legs add 2-1/2" at each side, giving a 41"-wide headboard, while the two 75"-long storage bases flank a mattress and stand 15-3/4" deep each. 41" will not span the finished bed's width. The plan does not resolve this and the prose does not guess.

Verified and used in the prose: each storage base is 75" long with two 3/4" ends and two 3/4" dividers, leaving three bays of exactly 24", which is why a drawer box built 23" outside (21-1/2" fronts/backs captured between 14" sides, confirmed by the 14" x 23" bottoms) is exactly 1" narrower than its opening, and why a 23-3/4" face leaves a 1/8" reveal each side. Box geometry confirms the 14" sides run full and the 21-1/2" fronts/backs sit between them.

ops.body: From the 3/4" plywood sheet cut two headboard/footboard panels at 26-3/4" x 36". Rip the two strip sheets to 15-3/4" wide and crosscut four storage base sides at 75" and eight ends/dividers at 14-7/8". From the 1/4" plywood cut two storage base backs at 16-1/4" x 75" and twelve drawer bottoms at 14" x 23". From the Pine 1x3 cut two panel top trims at 36" and 14 drawer support blocks at 7"; that is two 8 ft boards, with two more 1x3 held back for mattress slats. From the Pine 1x2 cut two panel bottom trims at 36" — that is the 1x2's only job, so ONE 8 ft board covers it, not three. The optional mouldings come from the two 8 ft decorative moulding sticks: four long at 30" and four short at 16-3/4", all ends mitered 45° and not parallel, which packs as two long plus two short per stick. Cut two headboard legs at 49-1/4" and two footboard legs at 30-1/4" from the 2x3; four frame rails at 70" and four frame ends at 10" from the 2x4; two mattress cleats at 75" from the 2x2; 24 drawer box sides at 14" and twelve fronts/backs at 21-1/2" from the seven 1x4; and twelve drawer faces at 23-3/4" from the four 1x8.

Lay out each storage base before anything is fastened, because this is the measurement the whole bed turns on. A base is 75" long with a 3/4" end panel at each end and two 3/4" dividers between them, which leaves three bays of exactly 24" — and 24" is why a drawer box built 23" wide is the 1" narrower that bottom-mount slides want. Mount a slide member on each face that borders an opening: the inside face of both ends and BOTH faces of every divider, all of them set back 3/4" from the front edge so an applied drawer face has somewhere to sit. Do this now while the box is open on all sides; once the sides and the plywood back are on you are reaching in blind with a driver you cannot see. Set each pair level to each other with a square and mark the height on both the end and the divider from the same reference edge, or the drawer will run downhill.

Build the 2x4 frame for each base from two 70" rails and two 10" ends, then set the plywood sides, the two 14-7/8" end panels and the two 14-7/8" dividers onto it, joining them with pocket holes or 1-1/4" brads and glue. Keep every divider dead square to the sides as you fasten it — a divider that leans takes both of its slides out of parallel and you will feel it in two drawers, not one. Check the base's diagonals with a long tape and hold it square while you glue and brad the 1/4" plywood back on with 3/4" brads: the back is what fixes the box square permanently, so it is the last thing on and it goes on to a box you have just measured, not one you assume. Build the second base the same way and compare the two bay-by-bay before moving on.

Each drawer box is two 14" 1x4 sides running the full depth with a 21-1/2" front and back captured between them, which makes the box exactly 23" wide outside — the 14" x 23" plywood bottoms are the proof of that reading, and 23" in a 24" bay is the 1" of total clearance the slides need. Glue and brad each corner with 1-1/4" brads, then glue and pin the 1/4" bottom onto the underside with 3/4" brads, checking the diagonals of every box before the glue grabs. Measure your actual bays first rather than trusting the numbers blindly: if a bay came out 23-7/8" or 24-1/8", that box needs to follow it, because a bottom-mount slide is unforgiving in both directions — too tight and the drawer binds, too loose and it racks in the opening. Build all twelve to the same setup so they are interchangeable.

Fit the drawer member of each slide to its box, slide all twelve boxes home, and check that each runs freely to the back before any face goes on. Then hang the faces: a 23-3/4" face in a 24" opening leaves 1/8" of reveal at each side, so cut spacers to that thickness and rest the face on them while you fix it rather than eyeballing the gap. Fasten from inside the drawer box through its front into the back of the face so nothing shows, using screws short enough not to break through a 3/4" face. Work along one base setting all six faces before you judge them, since the eye reads the whole row of gaps at once and one face 1/16" out is obvious in a line of six. Add the knobs or handles last, marking them all from a single template.

Each loaded storage base carries real weight over a 75" span, so build a support under each one, set flush to the back of the base and to the headboard end, using the 2x4 offcuts and pocket screws. The plan cuts 14 drawer support blocks at 7" from 1x3 scrap but never says where they go; the sensible use is to pack them behind or beneath the slide runs where a base needs bearing, and their placement is genuinely left to you. Set both bases on the floor where the bed will live, get them level with each other, and shim the supports rather than relying on the floor being flat — a base that sits low at one end puts every drawer in it out of level.

Stand the headboard and footboard at the ends of the two storage bases and screw them to the base ends, checking the whole assembly for square across the diagonals before the last screws. Be aware of one number that does not close: the panels are 36" wide and the 2x3 legs add 2-1/2" at each side, so the headboard is 41" across, which is narrower than the mattress plus the two 15-3/4" bases it has to span — measure your own mattress and bases and work out how the headboard meets them before you drill. Screw a 75" 2x2 cleat along the inside face of each base, both at the same height, then cut mattress slats from the two 1x3 held back for the purpose and screw them down across the cleats so they cannot shift; the plan gives no slat length, count or spacing, so size them to the gap between your cleats and space them to whatever your mattress maker specifies. Sand everything, finish, and load the drawers.
